titleStmt.title: Understanding the physics of coherent LiDAR

analytic.title: Understanding the physics of coherent LiDAR

application.desc: GROBID - A machine learning software for extracting information from scholarly documents

div.head: Introduction
div.p: Coherent LiDAR (Light Detecting And Ranging) is a promising 3D imaging technology that provides significant advantages over more traditional LiDAR systems. In addition to being immune to ambient light [1], coherent LiDAR directly measures the velocity of moving objects by sensing Doppler shift of light [1,2], and can achieve exceptional depth accuracies. Due to these attributes, it is becoming popular for autonomous vehicle applications. Coherent LiDAR is also the ranging technique of choice for a variety of groups attempting to develop fully solid-state LiDAR systems [3,4,5,6].

The goal of this manuscript is to explain the basic physics of coherent LiDAR with rigorous derivations from first principles. In particular, we strive to answer two key questions about coherent LiDAR:
1. What is the sensitivity of coherent LiDAR, i.e. how many photons need to be collected to detect a target?
2. What is the collection efficiency of coherent LiDAR, i.e. how many photons are actually returned from a target?

div.head: Architectures
div.p: Amplitude modulated LiDAR systems use direct detection, where scattered light from a target is directly detected by a photodiode or other photodetector. Although direct detection is simple to implement, system performance is typically limited by detector noise and ambient light.

In contrast, coherent LiDAR systems use coherent detection, where the weak scattered signal is mixed with a strong local oscillator signal on a detector. The signal is significantly amplified by this mixing process, allowing relatively noisy detectors to be used. Due to the use of coherent detection, a coherent LiDAR system can directly measure the velocity of moving objects by sensing the Doppler shift of light. Furthermore, since a coherent detector can only detect light that is close in frequency to the local oscillator light, coherent LiDAR is virtually immune to interferene from ambient light.

As illustrated in figure 1, coherent LiDAR systems typically use one of two schemes: the frequency-modulated continuous-wave (FMCW) scheme, and the phase-encoded scheme. The physical systems are almost identical to each other; the only difference is in the modulation scheme. In an FMCW system, a linearly chirped laser is used as both the transitted signal and local oscillator. Meanwhile, in a phase-encoded system, the transmitted signal is modulated in phase, while the un-modulated laser is used as the local oscillator. For simplicity, the LiDAR systems shown in figure 1 use balanced detectors. Although more sophisticated implementations may replace the balanced detector with a full coherent receiver, and introduce additional components to sample multiple poplarizations, this does not have a major influence on the basic physics of operation.

div.head: Frequency-modulated continuous-wave (FMCW)
div.p: The FMCW scheme uses a linearly chirped laser for both the transmitter and local oscillator [1,2], as illustrated in figure 2. In the case of a static target, the received signal is a simply a time-delayed version of the transmitted signal. By mixing the transmitted and received signals on an optical heterodyne receiver, the frequency difference between the transmitted and received signal can be extracted. The frequency difference is proportional to the round-trip travel time, and is therefore a measure of the target range.
If the target is moving, the received signal will have an additional frequency shift ∆f Doppler proportional to the velocity v due to the Doppler effect:
div.formula: ∆f Doppler ≈ 2v λ ,(1)
div.p: Here, λ is the wavelength of light.

To measure both the range and velocity to a target, triangular modulation is commonly used, where an up-chirp is immediately followed by a down-chirp. The measured frequency differences ∆f up and ∆f down during the up-and downchirps respectively can then be used to compute the range d,
div.formula: d = c (∆f up + ∆f down ) 4r(2)
div.p: and the velocity v,
div.formula: v = λ (∆f up -∆f down ) 4 .(3)
div.p: Here, c is the speed of light, and r is the chirp ramp rate.
Figure 2: Frequency-modulated continuous-wave LiDAR scheme. By following an up-chirp with a down-chirp, it is possible to extract both the range and velocity of a moving object.

div.head: Phase-encoded
div.p: In the phase-encoded scheme, the transmitted beam is phase-modulated with a pseudo-random sequence of bits [7]. The received signal is detected on a coherent detector, and then digitally cross-correlated with the transmitted bit-stream to determine the time delay and therefore range to the target. Although this approach is simpler to implement than the FMCW approach which requires a highly linear frequency chirp, there are a several challenges that must be overcome to create a successful system. First, the Doppler shifts can be very large, reaching up to 50-100 MHz for automotive LiDARs, severely interfering with the cross-correlation step. Careful selection of pseudo-random sequences and additional digital signal processing is needed to handle Doppler shifted returns [7]. Furthermore, backreflections can elevate the noise floor, and need to be digitally subtracted before taking any cross correlations.

div.head: Sensitivity of coherent detectors
div.p: The sensitivity of amplitude-modulated LiDARs is typically limited by system imperfections such as detector dark current, amplifier noise, and ambient light. Although single-photon sensitive systems are permitted by the laws of physics, no commercially available system approaches this level of performance.

In contrast, it is quite straightforward to create a coherent LiDAR system that operates at the fundamental quantum limit of noise. This is achieved when the shot noise of the local oscillator light dominates over all other noise sources in the system, and is typically referred to as the shot noise limited regime [8,9]. Shot-noise limited detection can be achieved even with relatively noisy photodetectors, as long as the local oscillator light is powerful enough to overcome any detector noise.

A key point to be made, however, is that the fundamental quantum limit for noise is substantially higher for coherent LiDAR systems than amplitudemodulated systems. Amplitude-modulated LiDAR is fundamentally limited by thermal noise. At the optical frequencies used by LiDAR systems, thermal noise is essentially zero, and it is possible to robustly measure range by detecting individual photons (for example, with superconducting nanowire detectors [10]). In contrast, coherent LiDAR systems are limited by the shot noise of the local oscillator light, and require on the order of a few dozen photons for robust detection.

In this section, we will derive the single-to-noise ratio of coherent LiDAR systems as a function of the number of collected photons. For convenience, we will focus on the FMCW scheme, although phase-encoded schemes will have very similar results.

div.head: Balanced optical heterodyne receiver
div.p: Consider the ideal balanced optical heterodyne receiver shown in figure 3. The receiver combines light from the two inputs A and B on a 3 dB coupler, which is then detected by two photodiodes. The difference in photocurrent between these two photodiodes is given as the output of the receiver.
We will consider the simple case where the two inputs are single-frequency signals at slightly different frequencies. The input optical fields E A and E B would then be
div.formula: E A = A cos ω A t(4)
E B = B cos ω B t,(5)
div.p: with amplitudes A and B and optical frequencies ω A and ω B respectively. For convenience, we will also define the time-averaged photocurrents of the two inputs
div.formula: i A = R E 2 A = RA 2 cos 2 ω A t = RA 2 2 (6) i B = R E 2 B = RB 2 cos 2 ω B t = RB 2 2 , (7
div.p: where R is the responsivity of the photodiodes in the balanced detector, and • denotes the average value.
Our goal is to compute the photocurrents generated by the two photodiodes in the balanced detector. The electric field E 1 impinging on the first photodiode is given by
div.formula: E 1 = 1 √ 2 (E A + E B ) = 1 √ 2 (A cos ω A t + B cos ω B t).(8)
div.p: The instantaneous power E 2 1 is then
div.formula: E 2 1 = 1 2 A 2 cos 2 ω A t + B 2 cos 2 ω B t + 2AB cos ω A t cos ω B t .(9)
div.p: Making use of the identities
div.formula: cos 2 θ = 1 2 + 1 2 cos 2θ (10) cos θ cos φ = 1 2 cos(θ + φ) + 1 2 cos(θ -φ),(11)
div.p: our expression for the instantaneous power becomes
div.formula: E 2 1 = 1 4 A 2 + B 2 + A 2 cos 2ω A t + B 2 cos 2ω B t(12)
+ 2AB cos(ω A + ω B )t + 2AB cos(ω A -ω B )t . (13
div.p: The photocurrent i 1 (t) is related to the instantaneous power E 2 1 by the responsivity R, and is given by
div.formula: i 1 (t) = R 4 A 2 + B 2 + 2AB cos(ω A -ω B ) .(14)
div.p: Here, we have dropped all high-frequency terms since the photodiode can at best respond to frequencies in the gigahertz range. Rewriting this expression in terms of the input photocurrents i A and i B yields
div.formula: i 1 (t) = 1 2 (i A + i B ) + √ i A i B cos (ω A -ω B ) t.(15)
div.p: Similarly, the field E 2 impinging on the second photodiode is
div.formula: E 2 = 1 √ 2 (E A -E B ) = 1 √ 2 (A cos ω A t -B cos ω B t),(16)
div.p: and the corresponding photocurrent is
div.formula: i 2 (t) = 1 2 (i A + i B ) - √ i A i B cos (ω A -ω B ) t.(17)
div.p: The output current i(t) is the difference between the individual photocurrents i 1 (t) and i 2 (t), which will therefore be
div.formula: i(t) = i 1 (t) -i 2 (t) = 2 √ i A i B cos(ω A -ω B )t.(18)
div.p: Thus, an optical heterodyne detector directly measures the frequency difference ω A -ω B between the two input beams. The FMCW scheme makes direct use of this to measure the range to targets.

div.head: Shot-noise limited detection
div.p: In a coherent LiDAR system, an optical heterodyne receiver is used to detect photons scattered from the target. Local oscillator light is fed into input A of the heterodyne receiver, and the scattered light is fed into input B. The scattered signal is typically extremely weak, and may consist of as few as a couple dozen photons. The local oscillator photocurrent R E 2 A = i LO will thus typically be orders of magnitude stronger than the signal photocurrent R E 2 B = i sig . Ideally, the optical heterodyne receiver is operated in the shot-noise limited regime, where local oscillator shot noise dominates over all other noise sources. In this regime, the signal-to-noise ratio depends only upon the number of collected photons, as we will show below.

div.head: FMCW signal
div.p: If we ignore all noise sources, we can see from equation 18 that the idealized balanced detector output in an FMCW system will be
div.formula: i(t) = 2 i LO i sig cos(ω A -ω B )t, (19
div.p: where the difference frequency ∆ω = ω A -ω B is proportional to the range to the target. This FMCW signal can be extremely weak, and is typically detected in the frequency domain by digitizing the detector output and taking a fast Fourier transform (FFT).

Our goal is to determine the signal-to-noise ratio of this signal as a function of the number of collected photoelectrons, which in turn depends upon the signal photocurrent i sig and integration time T . We can model the finite integration time by applying a window function w(t) to the detector output i(t) to obtain the windowed detector output i w (t), i w (t) = i(t) w(t).
Next, to compute the signal-to-noise ratio in the frequency domain, we need to calculate the Fourier transform I w (f ) of the windowed detector output. The convolution theorem states that
where I(f ) and W (f ) are the Fourier transforms of i(t) and w(t) respectively, and * is the convolution operator
We first turn our attention to computing the detector photocurrent term I(f ), which is given in the time domain by equation 19. Since the Fourier transform of cos ωt is
div.formula: ˆ∞ -∞ cos ωt e -2πjf t dt = 1 2 δ f - ω 2π + δ f + ω 2π ,(23)
div.p: the Fourier transform of i(t) is given by
div.formula: I(f ) = i LO i sig δ (f -f A + f B ) + δ (f + f A -f B ) .(24)
div.p: Here, we have defined
div.formula: f A = ω A /2π and f B = ω B /2π.
div.p: Next, we turn our attention to computing the Fourier transform W (f ) of the window function w(t). In any practical system, the window function is carefully be chosen to optimize system performance. Due to the presence of unavoidable backreflections within the LiDAR system, window functions that minimize sidelobes such as the Hann window are generally preferred. For the purposes of our current discussion, however, we will assume that we have a rectangular window function as shown in figure 4, which maximizes the signalto-noise ratio. In terms of the rectangular function
div.formula: rect(t) = 1, |t| < 1 2 0, otherwise,(25)
div.p: the rectangular window w(t) can be written as
div.formula: w(t) = rect t T .(26)
div.p: Figure 4: Rectangular window applied to the FMCW signal.
Since the Fourier transform of rect t is
div.formula: ˆ∞ -∞ rect t e -2πjf t dt = sin πf πf = sinc f,(27)
div.p: the Fourier transform W (f ) of the window function is therefore
div.formula: W (f ) = T sinc T f. (28
div.p: Combining equations 21, 24, and 28, we find that that the Fourier transform of the windowed detector function I w (f ) is
div.formula: I w (f ) = T i LO i sig sinc T (f -f A + f B ) + sinc T (f + f A -f B ) .(29)
div.p: The two-sided energy spectral density is therefore
div.formula: |I w (f )| 2 = T 2 i LO i sig sinc 2 T (f -f A + f B ) + sinc 2 T (f + f A -f B )(30)
div.p: where we have ignored any cross-terms by assuming that |f A -f B | 1/T .

div.head: Shot noise
div.p: Now that we have an understanding of the signal strength in an FMCW LiDAR system, our next step is to determine the noise floor set by the photocurrent shot noise. The shot noise depends upon the sum i 1 (t) + i 2 (t) of the photocurrents generated by the photodiodes, which is approximately equal to the local oscillator photocurrent i LO in a typical LiDAR system where i LO i sig . The two-sided power spectral density of the shot noise is therefore
div.formula: S(f ) = q i LO , (31
div.p: where q is the charge of an electron.

To compute the signal-to-noise ratio, we need to find the expected energy spectral density |I n (f )| 2 of the windowed shot-noise. This is given by the convolution of the noise power spectral density S(f ) with the energy spectral density |W (f )| 2 of the windowing function:
For a detailed derivation of this result, please see Appendix A.
Using our expressions 28 and 31 and for W (f ) and S(f ) respectively, we find that the energy spectral density is
div.formula: |I n (f )| 2 = ˆ∞ -∞ S(f -f ) |W (f )| 2 df = ˆ∞ -∞ q i LO T 2 sinc 2 T f df = q i LO T,(33)
div.p: where we have made use of
div.formula: ˆ∞ -∞ sinc 2 x dx = 1. (34

div.head: Signal-to-noise ratio
div.p: Since the FMCW signal is uncorrelated with the shot noise, the total expected energy spectral density
div.formula: |I t (f )| 2 is simply the sum of the FMCW signal |I w (f )| 2 and the shot noise |I n (f )| 2 , |I t (f )| 2 = |I w (f )| 2 + |I n (f )| 2 ,(35)
div.p: which we have plotted below in figure 5.

Figure 5: Expected energy spectral density for an FMCW LiDAR signal. There is a strong peak at the difference frequency f A -f B , corresponding to the range to the target. The signal-to-noise ratio is exactly equal to the number of signal photoelectrons N .
To find the signal-to-noise ratio, we take the ratio of the FMCW signal and shot noise spectral densities. Using equations 30 and 33, this yields
div.formula: |I w (f )| 2 |I n (f )| 2 = N sinc 2 T (f -f A + f B ) + sinc 2 T (f + f A -f B ) , (36
div.p: where N is the number of signal photoelectrons collected during the acquisition time T ,
div.formula: N = i sig T q . (37
div.p: Since sinc 2 x ≤ 1, the signal-to-noise ratio SNR in the frequency domain is exactly equal to the number of signal photoelectrons N :
div.formula: SNR = N. (38
div.p: This is the basis of the common claim that coherent LiDAR systems have "single-photon sensitivity". However, a signal-to-noise ratio of one is not nearly high enough for robust detection, and in practice coherent LiDAR systems require several dozen photons for a detection.

div.head: Collection efficiency of coherent LiDAR
div.p: Coherent LiDAR systems have a very fundamental and key limitation: they can only detect light in a single optical mode. This is immediately obvious if we consider a coherent LiDAR system constructed using single-mode optical fibers: only scattered light that makes its way back into the guided mode of the collecting fiber can be detected. However, this also turns out to be true no matter how the coherent LiDAR is constructed [11], even if only free-space optical components such as lenses and beamsplitters are used! This obviously has severe implications for the collection efficiency of a coherent LiDAR system. In an amplitude modulated LiDAR system that uses direct detection, we can trivially increase the collection efficiency by increasing the size of the detector aperture. However, in a coherent LiDAR system, increasing the size of the detector aperture does not necessarily improve the collection efficiency [12].
As we will show later, the average optical power P collected by an ideal coherent LiDAR from a diffuse target is

Here, I is the intensity of the scattered light at the surface of the target, and λ is the wavelength of light. Since the wavelength of light is only ∼ 10 -6 m, only a tiny fraction of the scattered light is collected by the LiDAR system. A particularly striking feature of equation 39 is that the optical power depends only upon the scattered light intensity I, and therefore the intensity of the illuminating beam. Many coherent LiDARS employ a monostatic configuration, where the transmitted and collected light share the same optical path. In the case of an ideal monostatic LiDAR, the average optical power P becomes
div.formula: P = λ 2 P s 2πA eff(40)
div.p: where P s is the total power of the scattered light, and A eff is the effective area of the beam. The signal strength is inversely proportional to the beam area, indicating that tightly focusing the transmit and collection beams is critical to achieving high performance in a coherent LiDAR system. The effective beam area of several common beam shapes is listed below.
div.formula: Type Intensity I(x, y) Effective area (A eff ) Flat-top 1, x 2 + y 2 < r 2 0, otherwise. πr 2 Gaussian exp - 2(x 2 + y 2 ) w 2 πw 2

div.head: Setup
div.p: Since most modern coherent LiDARs are based on fiber-optics or other integrated photonic waveguides, we will study the couping efficiency of scattered light into a single-mode waveguide.

Figure 6: Collection optics for a typical coherent LiDAR. Scattered light is focused using a lens, and coupled into a single-mode waveguide via a coupler. This coupler may be a grating coupler in the case of a integrated photonic waveguide, or possibly just a cleaved optical fiber in the case of a fiber-optic based system.

Our goal is to determine the expected power coupled into the input waveguide, which will ultimately allow for an understanding of the signal strength and thus the useful range of a coherent LIDAR. In particular, we will focus on the case of diffuse scattering surfaces.

div.head: Power in input waveguide
div.p: Our first step is to express the recieved power in terms of a field overlap integral. Consider a closed surface S that encapsulates the lens and waveguide coupler: The surface S is chosen so that only the front and back faces S 1 and S 2 have non-negligible fields.

We will denote the scattered electric and magnetic fields as E and H. We will also make use of the modal fields E m and H m , which are the fields produced by injecting the desired waveguide mode back into the system.

To find the power in the waveguide mode, we can perform a mode overlap integral over surface S 1 , as illustrated in figure 8. We choose our local coordinate Figure 7: To compute the power coupled into the waveguide, we will integrate the fields over surface S. The surface is chosen such that only the front and back faces S 1 and S 2 have non-negligible fields.

Figure 8: The power in the desired waveguide mode can be found using a mode overlap integral. The scattered light propagates through the waveguide in the +z direction, and the surface S 1 is located at z = 0. system such that S 1 is located in the z = 0 plane, and the scattered light propagates in the +z direction.
The modal fields E m and H m in the vicinity of surface S 1 are simply the fields of the waveguide mode propagating in the -z direction (i.e. away from the output). These have the form
div.formula: E m (x, y, z) = E m (x, y)e jβz(41)
H m (x, y, z) = H m (x, y)e jβz , s(42)
div.p: where β is the propagation constant of the waveguide. We will assume that the waveguide is reciprocal, lossless, and has reflection symmetry across some plane perpendicular to ẑ. The transverse components of the modal fields E m and H m can then be taken to be purely real (see Appendix B). For convenience, we further assume that the modal fields are normalized to unit power:

To determine the amplitude a of the scattered light coupled into the waveguide, we will use a mode overlap integral over the z = 0 plane. From [13], the amplitude of the waveguide mode propagating in the +z direction (i.e. towards the output) is given by
div.formula: a = 1 N z=0 (E m × H -E × H m ) • ẑ dx dy.(44)
div.p: The normalization constant N is
div.formula: N = -2 z=0 (E m × H m ) • ẑ dx dy. (45
div.p: We can simply our expression for N by making use of the fact that the transverse modal fields are purely real and normalized to unit power, yielding
Since the z = 0 plane coincides with the surface S 1 , and ẑ dx dy = dA, we can rewrite our expression for the output amplitude a as
div.formula: S1 (E m × H -E × H m ) • dA. (47
div.p: Finally, since the modal fields are power normalized, the collected power P is given by

div.head: Applying reciprocity
div.p: We now have an expression (47) for the output amplitude a in terms of the scattered fields coupled into the waveguide. Unfortunately, this expression is difficult to use directly, since it requires us to model the propagation of scattered light through the lens and the waveguide coupler. We would much prefer to have a field overlap integral over surface S 2 immediately adjacent to the scattering surface, where we can neglect everything except the coherence properties of the scattered light. We will achieve this by making use of the reciprocity theorem.
For a closed surface S that encloses no sources, the Lorentz reciprocity theorem [14] states that
Earlier, we assumed that the modal fields E m and H m are zero everywhere on S except for the front and back surfaces S 1 and S 2 . This allows us to rewrite (49) as
div.formula: S1 (E m × H -E × H m ) • dS = - S2 (E m × H -E × H m ) • dS.(50)
div.p: The term on the left side is exactly the same as the mode overlap integral (47) for the output amplitude a, except for a factor of 1 4 . The output amplitude is thus given by the overlap integral
div.formula: S2 (E × H m -E m × H) • dS. (51
div.p: over surface S 2 , which is adjacent to the scattering surface. At this point in our discussion, it is convenient to define the efficiency η of the optical system as the fraction of modal power reaching the target surface S 2 :
In a properly designed system, η will be close to unity. If η < 1, we will see that this is manifested as a reduction in the collected power.

div.head: "Flat surface" approximation
div.p: We can typically choose the integration surface S 2 to be flat on wavelength length scales. We will use this fact to considerably simplify our field overlap integral (51). Consider a locally flat patch of S 2 :

Figure 9: We assume that our integration surface S 2 is locally flat. In addition, we assume that the modal fields are roughly collimated, with an incidence angle of approximately θ with respect to the surface S 2 .

We have chosen our local coordinate system such that z = 0 on the surface S 2 , and the surface normal ẑ points towards the scattering surface. In addition, we assume that the modal fields E m , H m are roughly collimated in this patch of S 2 with an incidence angle of θ with respect to the surface. Our goal is to simplify the overlap integral (51), which can be written in this locally flat region as
div.formula: a = 1 4 z=0 (E × H m -E m × H) • ẑ dx dy.(53)
div.p: To do so, we will need to decompose the fields into their constituent plane waves. The scattered fields E and H can be written in terms of their plane wave components as
div.formula: E(r) = ∞ -∞ Ê(k x , k y ) e -jk•r dk x dk y(54)
H(r) = 1 Z 0 ∞ -∞ k × Ê(k x , k y ) e -jk•r dk x dk y .(55)
div.p: Here, r = (x, y, z) is the position, the wavevector k is given by
div.formula: k = k x , k y , 4π 2 λ 2 -k 2 x -k 2 y ,(56)
div.p: k = k/|k| is the propagation direction, Z 0 is the impedance of free space, and λ is the wavelength of light. We can see from equation (56) that the scattered waves only propagate in the -z direction, as desired.
Similarly, the modal fields E m and H m can be decomposed as
div.formula: E m (r) = ∞ -∞ Êm (k x , k y ) e -jkm•r dk x dk y (57) H m (r) = 1 Z 0 ∞ -∞ km × Êm (k x , k y ) e -jkm•r dk x dk y ,(58)
div.p: where the wavevector k m is given by
div.formula: k m = k x , k y , 4π 2 λ 2 -k 2 x -k 2 y .(59)
div.p: Note that the modal fields propagate in the +z direction, in contrast to the scattered fields. Now, recall that the overlap intgegral of two functions f (x) and g(x) can be written in terms of their Fourier transforms f (k) and ĝ(k) as
due to Plancherel's theorem. Applying (60) and the plane wave decompositions (54) and (58) to the first term in the overlap integral (53) yields
div.formula: 1 4 z=0 (E × H m ) • ẑ dx dy = 1 4 Z 0 ∞ -∞ Ê(-k x , -k y ) × km (k x , k y ) × Êm (k x , k y ) • ẑ dk x dk y .(61)
div.p: If we make use of the vector triple product a
div.formula: × (b × c) = b(a • c) -c(a • b), our expression becomes 1 4 z=0 (E × H m ) • ẑ dx dy = 1 4 Z 0 ∞ -∞ Ê(-k x , -k y ) • Êm (k x , k y ) km (k x , k y ) • ẑ -Ê(-k x , -k y ) • km (k x , k y ) Êm (k x , k y ) • ẑ dk x dk y .(62)
div.p: However, we can see from (56) and (59) that km (k x , k y ) = -k(-k x , -k y ), and the plane wave amplitude Ê(k x , k y ) is always perpendicular to its propagation direction k(k x , k y ), so the second term in (62) disappears. We are left with
div.formula: 1 4 z=0 (E × H m ) • ẑ dx dy = 1 4 Z 0 ∞ -∞ Ê(-k x , -k y ) • Êm (k x , k y ) km (k x , k y ) • ẑ dk x dk y .(63)
div.p: We now make use of our assumption that the modal beam is roughly collimated with an incidence angle of θ, which allows us to approximate
div.formula: km (k x , k y ) • ẑ ≈ cos θ. (64
div.p: Our final expression in the spatial frequency domain is then
div.formula: 1 4 z=0 (E × H m ) • ẑ dx dy = 1 4 Z 0 ∞ -∞ Ê(-k x -k y ) • Êm (k x , k y ) cos θ dk x dk y .(65)
div.p: Transforming back to real space, we obtain
div.formula: 1 4 z=0 (E × H m ) • ẑ dx dy = 1 4 Z 0 ∞ -∞ E • E m cos θ dx dy (66)
div.p: Similarly, the second term in the overlap integral (53) becomes
div.formula: z=0 (E m × H) • ẑ dx dy = 1 4 Z 0 ∞ -∞ E • E m cos θ dx dy(67)
div.p: The overlap integral (53) for the output amplitude a is thus approximately
div.formula: a = 1 2 Z 0 S2 E • E m cos θ dS(68)
div.p: This expression will be accurate as long as the integration surface S 2 is locally smooth on the wavelength scale, and the modal beam is roughly collimated over regions the size of several wavelengths.

div.head: Uniform illumination
div.p: The expected value of the recieved power depends upon the statistical properties of the scattered electric field E. To make the problem tractable, we first consider the simple case of a flat and uniform scattering surface that is illuminated uniformly across the entire surface. More precisely, our assumptions are the following:
1. S 2 is a completely flat surface. As in the previous section, we choose our coordinate system such that S 2 coincides with the z = 0 plane.
2. The modal field is roughly collimated, i.e. cos θ is approximately constant across all of S 2 .
3. The modal field E m has a fixed polarization p and has a slowly varying amplitude across S 2 . The modal electric field on the z = 0 plane is then of the form
div.formula: E m (x, y, 0) = ψ(x, y) e -j(kxx+kyy) p (69
div.p: where the slowly varying scalar amplitude ψ(x, y) is constant over wavelength length scales, and |p| 2 = 1.

4. The scattered field E has uniform intensity and coherence width over S 2 . More precisely, the autocorrelation function W(r 1 , r 2 ) of the electric field E(r) over S 2 only depends on the difference r 1 -r 2 in the positions.
Explicitly,
where • indicates the expected value, and † is the complex transpose. In general, the electric field autocorrelation W will be a 3 × 3 tensor.
Some of these assumptions will be relaxed later.
Using the assumptions 1 -3, our expression (68) for the output amplitude a becomes
div.formula: a = cos θ 2 Z 0 ∞ -∞ (E(x, y, 0) • p) ψ(x, y) e -j(kxx+kyy) dx dy. (71
div.p: The expected value of the collected power P is then
where p = p * . Making use of our definition (70) for the electric field autocorrelation W yields
div.formula: P = cos 2 θ 4 Z 2 0 ∞ -∞ p † W(x 1 -x 2 , y 1 -y 2 ) p e -j(kx(x1-x2)+ky(y1-y2)) × ψ(x 1 , y 1 ) ψ * (x 2 , y 2 ) dx 1 dx 2 dy 1 dy 2 (73)
div.p: To further simplify our expression, we define the scalar autocorrelation function ρ(x, y) as
div.formula: ρ(x, y) = cos θ 2 Z 0 p † W(x, y) p e -j(kxx+kyy) ,(74)
div.p: and our expression for the collected power becomes
div.formula: P = cos θ 2 Z 0 ∞ -∞ ρ(x 1 -x 2 , y 1 -y 1 ) ψ(x 1 , y 1 ) ψ * (x 2 , y 2 ) dx 1 dx 2 dy 1 dy 2 (75)
div.p: The scalar autocorrelation function ρ(x, y) vanishes for large x and y; this length scale is known as the coherence width. Since ψ(x, y) is a slowly varying amplitude, ρ will typically vanish on much shorter length scales than on which ψ varies. This allows us to greatly simplify our expression for P . More precisely, if ρ(x, y) ≈ 0 for any x 2 + y 2 > L, and ψ(x, y) is effectively constant on length scale L, we can approximate ρ as a delta function:
where the characteristic power P 0 of a single speckle is
div.formula: P 0 = ∞ -∞ ρ(x, y) dx dy = cos θ 2 Z 0 p †   ∞ -∞ W(x, y) e -j(kxx+kyy) dx dy   p (77)
div.p: However, the term in the brackets is equal to the spatial Fourier transform of the autocorrelation tensor,
div.formula: Ŵ(k x , k y ) = ∞ -∞ W(x, y) e -jkxx-jkyy dx dy,(78)
div.p: which allows us to write the characteristic power P 0 more concisely as
div.formula: P 0 = cos θ 2 Z 0 p † Ŵ(k x , k y ) p. (79
div.p: Under this approximation, (75) becomes
div.formula: P = cos θ 2 Z 0 ∞ -∞ P 0 δ(x 1 -x 2 ) δ(y 1 -y 2 ) ψ(x 1 , y 1 ) ψ * (x 2 , y 2 ) dx 1 dx 2 dy 1 dy 2 = P 0 cos θ 2 Z 0 ∞ -∞ |ψ(x, y)| 2 dx dy.(80)
div.p: Now, recall that our modal field is power normalized, and the power reaching the target surface S 2 is given by the efficiency η:
For our ansatz modal E-field (69), the corresponding H-field is given by
div.formula: H m (x, y, 0) = 1 Z 0 ψ(x, y) e -j(kxx+kyy) km × p,(81)
div.p: where km = k m /|k m | is the propagation direction, and the wavevector k m is given by (59). Substituting our ansatz (69) and (81) into our expression for the efficiency (52) yields
div.formula: η = 1 2 Z 0 Re p × km × p * • ẑ ∞ -∞ |ψ(x, y)| 2 dx dy = 1 2 Z 0 Re |p| 2 km • ẑ -(p • km )(p • ẑ) ∞ -∞ |ψ(x, y)| 2 dx dy = cos θ 2 Z 0 ∞ -∞ |ψ(x, y)| 2 dx dy(82)
div.p: where we have made use of our normalization |p| 2 = 1, the orthogonality of the polarization and propagation direction p • km = 0, and our definition of the incidence angle km • ẑ = cos θ.
We obtain our final result by substituting (82) into (80), which allows us to express the collected power in terms of the efficiency η of the system:
Thus, if we have a lossless system with η = 1, the expected collected power is exactly equal to the characteristic power P 0 of a single speckle in the scattered field.

div.head: Non-uniform illumination
div.p: In the previous section, we assumed that the scattered light is uniform in intensity across the entire scattering surface. This assumption is not true for most coherent LiDAR systems, which typically scan the scene with tightly focused laser beams. We can model the case of non-uniform illumination by allowing the speckle power P 0 to vary across the scattering surface. We will find it particularly illuminating to write the speckle power in terms of the scattered light intensity I(x, y) and the characteristic speckle area A 0 ,

The characteristic area A 0 of a speckle is a property of the scattering surface, which we will assume remains constant over the entire scattering surface. Meanwhile, the scattered light intensity I(x, y) is proportional to the intensity of the illuminating light, and can vary strongly with position. Under these assumptions, our expression 80 for the expected power P becomes
div.formula: P = A 0 cos θ 2 Z 0 ∞ -∞ I(x, y) |ψ(x, y)| 2 dx dy.(85)
div.p: The received power is therefore entirely dependent upon the overlap integral between the scattered light intensity I(x, y) and modal field |ψ(x, y)| 2 . For convenience, we define the power normalized modal field ψ(x, y) as
div.formula: ψ(x, y) = cos θ 2 Z 0 η ψ(x, y),(86)
div.p: which satisfies the property
Meanwhile, the total power of the scattered light P s is given by
div.formula: P s = ∞ -∞ I(x, y) dx dy,(88)
div.p: which can be used to define a normalized scattered light intensity Ī(x, y),
div.formula: Ī(x, y) = I(x, y) P s .(89)
div.p: Rewriting 85 in terms of the normalized quantities ψ(x, y) and Ī(x, y) yields our final expression for the expected power,
div.formula: P = η P s A 0 ∞ -∞ I(x, y) ψ(x, y) 2 dx dy.(90)

div.head: Monostatic LiDAR
div.p: Many coherent LiDAR designs are monostatic, sharing the transmit and receive paths. For example, a monostatic LiDAR may use the same collimator and optical fiber to both transmit and receive light. In the case of a monostatic LiDAR, the intensity distribution of the scattered light Ī(x, y) and the collection mode | ψ(x, y)| 2 will be identical, i.e.
If we define the effective area A eff of the laser beam as
div.formula: 1 A eff = ∞ -∞ Ī2 (x, y), dx dy,(92)
div.p: we obtain a very simple and intuitive expression for the expected power,
div.formula: P = η P s A 0 A eff .(93)
div.p: The collected power P can thus be understood as the fraction of scattered power P s that falls within a characteristic speckle area A 0 , multipled by the efficiency η of the collection optics.
We will now compute the effective beam area for several commonly encountered beam profiles.

div.head: Flat-top beam
div.p: A flat-top beam has an intensity profile of the form
div.formula: Ī(x, y) = 1 A beam 1, (x, y) ∈ Ω 0, otherwise,(94)
div.p: where A beam is the physical area of the beam. From equation 92, we can see that the effective beam area A eff is exactly equal to the physical area of the beam, since
div.formula: 1 A eff = ∞ -∞ Ī2 (x, y) dx dy = Ω 1 A 2 beam dx dy = 1 A beam . (95
div.p: Gaussian beam A Gaussian beam with beam radius w has an intensity profile of the form
To find the normalization N , we take advantage of the fact that the integral of Ī(x, y) should be unity:
div.formula: 1 = ∞ -∞ Ī(x, y) dx dy = ∞ -∞ N exp - 2(x 2 + y 2 ) w 2 dx dy = N π w 2 2 . (97
div.p: Thus, the normalization constant N = 2/πw 2 . Finding the effective area using equation 92 then yields
div.formula: 1 A eff = ∞ -∞ Ī2 (x, y) dx dy = 4 π 2 w 4 ∞ -∞ exp - 4(x 2 + y 2 ) w 2 . dx dy = 1 πw 2 . (98
div.p: We can conclude that the effective area of a Gaussian beam is simply
div.formula: A eff = πw 2 . (99

div.head: Ideal Lambertian scattering surface
div.p: We will now consider the case of Lambertian scattering, which is an idealized model of diffuse scattering. An Lambertian scatterer takes all incident light and scatters it in a cosine distribution, as illustrated in figure 10. The angular distribution of the scattered light is independent of the incidence angle of the incident light. Rather conveniently for us, an ideal blackbody emitter is a Lambertian emitter. In other words, the light scattered by a Lambertian scatterer is indistinguishable from blackbody radiation, provided that the scattered light is completely depolarized. We can thus directly apply previous results for the spatial coherence of blackbody radiation to Lambertian scattering. From [15], the electric field at the surface of a perfect blackbody is given by the superposition of planewaves
div.formula: E(r) = α A(û) exp(-jk 0 û • r) dΩ(100)
div.p: where û = sin θ cos φ x + sin θ sin φ ŷ + cos θ ẑ (101)

is the unit vector that specifies the propagation direction of a plane wave in spherical coordinates (φ, θ). The differential solid angle is given by dΩ = sin θ dθ dφ, and α is the 2π solid angle corresponding to the upper hemisphere.
The wavenumber k 0 in the medium is related to the wavelength λ by k 0 = 2π/λ. The vector amplitude A(û) of an individual planewave can be decomposed in terms of the s and p polarizations as
div.formula: A(û) = A s (û)ŝ + A p (û)p (102
div.p: where the polarization vectors ŝ and p are given by
The correlations between the amplitudes are
where a 0 is real and positive, and the directional Dirac delta function is defined as

div.head: Fourier transform of electric field autocorrelation
div.p: Fundamentally, we are interested in finding the characteristic power P 0 of a speckle. We can see from equation (79) that this requires us to take the spatial Fourier transform of the electric field autocorrelation tensor W, defined as
div.formula: Ŵ(k x , k y ) = ∞ -∞ W(x, y) e -jkxx-jkyy dx dy(78)
div.p: Meanwhile, from [15], the electric field autocorrelation tensor at the surface of a perfect blackbody is
div.formula: W(x, y) = a 0 α U 3 -ûû T e jkxx+jkyy dΩ.(108)
div.p: where U 3 is the 3×3 identity matrix. Our goal, then, is to change the integration variables in (108) to k x and k y so that it can be understood as an inverse spatial Fourier transform. We note that
div.formula: k x = k 0 sin θ cos φ(109)
k y = k 0 sin θ sin φ.(110)
div.p: The Jacobian J of the transformation (θ, φ) → (k x , k y ) is then given by
div.formula: J -1 =     ∂k x ∂θ ∂k x ∂φ ∂k y ∂θ ∂k y ∂φ     = k 0 cos θ cos φ -sin θ sin φ cos θ sin φ sin θ cos φ ,(111)
div.p: and the absolute value of the determinant is
div.formula: |J| -1 = k 2 0 cos θ sin θ cos 2 φ + cos θ sin θ sin 2 φ = k 2 0 cos θ sin θ.(112)
div.p: Applying this change of integration variables to (108) yields
div.formula: W(x, y) = a 0 k2 x +k 2 y <k 2 0 sin θ |J| U 3 -ûû T e jkxx+jkyy dk x dk y = a 0 k 2 0 k2 x +k 2 y <k 2 0 1 cos θ U 3 -ûû T e jkxx+jkyy dk x dk y .(113)
div.p: Applying the Fourier inversion theorem, we conclude that the spatial Fourier transform Ŵ(k x , k y ) of the autocorrelation tensor is
div.formula: Ŵ(k x , k y ) =    4π 2 a 0 k 2 0 1 cos θ U 3 -ûû T , k 2 x + k 2 y ≤ k 2 0 0, otherwise.(114)

div.head: Intensity of blackbody radiation
div.p: Our next step is to relate the normalization constant a 0 to the intensity of the blackbody radiation. We define the intensity I as the expected Poynting flux per unit area of the emitting surface:
To compute the intensity, we first need to find the magnetic field H of the blackbody radiation, which is given by
div.formula: H(r) = 1 Z 0 α (A s (û) û × ŝ + A p (û) û × p) exp(-jk 0 û • r) dΩ = 1 Z 0 α (-A s (û) p + A p (û) ŝ) exp(-jk 0 û • r) dΩ.(116)
div.p: Combining ( 100) and ( 115) -( 116) yields
Simplifying using the amplitude correlations (105) and (106), we obtain
div.formula: I = 1 2Z 0 Re    α dΩ 1 α dΩ 2 exp (-jk 0 (û 1 -û2 )) ẑ • A s (û 1 )A * s (û 2 )ŝ 1 × p2 + A p (û 1 )A * p (û 2 )ŝ 2 × p1    = 1 2Z 0 Re    α dΩ 1 α dΩ 2 exp (-jk 0 (û 1 -û2 )) a 0 δ(û 1 -û2 ) ẑ • (ŝ 1 × p2 + ŝ2 × p1 )    = a 0 Z 0 α dΩ 1 Re {ẑ • (ŝ 1 × p1 )} . (118) However, since (p × ŝ) • ẑ = û • ẑ = cos θ, I = a 0 Z 0 α cos θ 1 dΩ 1 .(119)
div.p: This integral over the 2π solid angle is
div.formula: α cos θ dΩ = 2π 0 dφ π/2 0 dθ sin θ cos θ = π,(120)
div.p: yielding our final expression for the intensity

div.head: Speckle power and area
div.p: We are now in a position to compute the characteristic power P 0 of a single speckle, which we defined as
div.formula: P 0 = cos θ 2 Z 0 p † Ŵ(k x , k y ) p.(79)
div.p: Substituting our result for the spatial Fourier transform of the autocorrelation tensor Ŵ(k x , k y ) for blackbody radiation (114) yields
div.formula: P 0 = cos θ 2 Z 0 4π 2 a 0 k 2 0 1 cos θ p † U 3 -ûû T p = 2π 2 a 0 k 2 0 Z 0 p † U 3 -ûû T p. (122
div.p: We can eliminate the normalization constant a 0 by making use of (121). In addition, we note that
This gives us our final expression for the speckle power of a Lambertian scatterer:

Here, λ is the wavelength of light, and the intensity I can be understood as the power emitted by the scattering surface per unit area of the surface. Meanwhile, the characteristic area A 0 of a speckle, which we defined in equation 84 as the speckle power P 0 divided by the intensity I, is given by
Changing variables τ = t -t in the inner integral yields The inner integral can be identified as the autocorrelation ŵ(τ ) of the window function w(t),
allowing us to simplify our expression to
div.formula: |Y (f )| 2 = ˆ∞ -∞ dτ x(τ ) ŵ(τ )e -2πjf τ .(138)
div.p: Applying the convolution theorem, we find that the expected energy spectral density is
where * is the convolution operator

div.head: Appendix B Modes of lossless waveguides
div.p: Theorem 1. Suppose we have a waveguide that is simultaneously:
div.formula: 1. Reciprocal.
div.p: 2. Lossless.
3. Has reflection symmetry across some plane perpendicular to ẑ.
Then the transverse field components of any guided mode of this waveguide will be purely real.
Proof. To show this, we will build upon the results given in reference [13].
Consider a guided mode of the waveguide, which is any solution to Maxwell's equations of the form
div.formula: E m (x, y, z) = E m (x, y)e jβz(143)
div.p: H m (x, y, z) = H m (x, y)e jβz (144) with a purely real propagation constant β. We denote the transverse components of the modal fields (i.e. the field components orthogonal to z) as E m,T and H m,T .
From reference [13], the following additional guided modes must exist:
1. Reflection symmetry implies that there also exists a mode with propagation constant -iβ and transverse modal fields E m,T and -H m,T .
2. Losslessness implies that there also exists a mode with propagation constant -iβ and transverse modal fields E * m,T and -H * m,T .

If our waveguide does not have mode degeneracy (i.e. no two waveguide modes share the same propagation constant), we can conclude that these two backwards propagating modes must be the same since they have the same propagation constant -jβ. This implies that the modal fields must be equal up to the some phase factor φ:
div.formula: E m,T = e jφ E * m,T(145)
div.p: Thus, e -jφ/2 E m,T and e -jφ/2 H m,T are purely real. In other words, if we have a waveguide that is reciprocal, lossless, has reflection symmetry, and lacks mode degeneracy, the transverse modal fields E m and H m of any propagating modes can be taken to be real. Waveguides with degenerate modes also satisfy this property, since they can be taken as the limiting case of distorted waveguides that lack mode degeneracy. For example, the fundamental mode of a circular core waveguide is degenerate due to rotational symmetry. However, a circular core waveguide can be understood as the limiting case of a sequence of elliptical core waveguides that gradually become more circular. Throughout this entire sequence, the fundamental mode will remain non-degenerate, and thus its transverse field components will remain purely real.

figure.head: Figure 1 :
figure.figDesc: Figure 1: The two basic coherent LiDAR architectures.

figure.head: Figure 10 :
figure.figDesc: Figure 10: In Lambertian scattering, the incident light is scattered into a cosine distribution. The percieved brightness of a Lambertian scatterer is independent of the viewing angle, and only depends upon the incident flux per unit area of the surface.

y)g(x -y)dy. (140)We can identify the integral in equation 139 as the energy spectral density |W (f )| 2 of the window function w(t), since|W (f )| 2 = ˆ∞ -∞ w(t)e -2πjf t dt ˆ∞ -∞ dt ˆ∞ -∞ dt w(t) w(t )e -2πjf (t-t ) = ˆ∞ -∞ dτ e -2πjf τ ˆ∞ -∞ dt w(t) w(t -τ ) = ˆ∞ -∞ ŵ(τ )e -2πjf τ dτ.(141)Thus, the expected energy spectral density |Y (f )| 2 is simply the power spectral density S(f ) of the random signal convolved with the energy spectral density |W (f )| 2 of the window function:|Y (f )| 2 = S(f ) * |W (f )| 2 .(142)
H m,T = e jφ H * m,T .(146)Rearranging yieldse -jφ/2 E m,T = e -jφ/2 E m,T *(147)e -jφ/2 H m,T = e -jφ/2 H m,T * .

ˆ∞ -∞ dτ y(t) y * (t -τ ) e -2πjf τ .(134)However, from our definition of y(t) in equation 130, we can expand y(t) y * (t -τ ) asy(t)y * (t -τ ) = w(t) w * (t -τ ) x(t) x * (t -τ )= w(t) w * (t -τ ) x(τ ).

row.cell: If we further change the order of integration, equation 134 becomes

div.p: Appendix A Spectral density of windowed random signals
Suppose we have a stationary random signal x(t). The autocorrelation x(τ ) is defined as
and the power spectral density S(f ) is
Here, • denotes the expected value. From the Wiener-Khinchin theorem, the autocorrelation x(τ ) and power-spectral density S(f ) are Fourier transform pairs, related by
Now, suppose we wish to take the Fourier transform of the random signal x(t) when it is windowed by some window function w(t). We define the windowed random signal y(t) as

and denote its Fourier transform as Y (f ). For simplicity, we will assume that the random process has zero mean, i.e. x(t) = 0. As a result, the expected value of Y (f ) will also be zero, since the Fourier transform and expection value operators commute with each other:
The expected energy spectral density |Y (f )|

analytic.title: Coherent solid-state LIDAR with silicon photonic optical phased arrays
512-element actively steered silicon phased array for low-power LIDAR
Long-range LiDAR and free-space data communication with high-performance optical phased arrays

monogr.title: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Quantum Electronics

analytic.title: Massively parallel coherent laser ranging using a soliton microcomb

monogr.title: Method and system for time separated quadrature detection of doppler effects in optical range measurements

analytic.title: Quantum theory of optical homodyne and heterodyne detection
Squeezing the local oscillator does not improve signal-to-noise ratio in heterodyne laser radar
Superconducting nanowire single-photon detectors: physics and applications
The antenna properties of optical heterodyne receivers
Heterodyne laser radar-SNR from a diffuse target containing multiple glints
Mode orthogonality in reciprocal and nonreciprocal waveguides

monogr.title: IEEE Transactions on Microwave Theory and Techniques

analytic.title: Partial polarization and electromagnetic spatial coherence of blackbody radiation emanating from an aperture
